content.text: Handling rules by data classification tier are a set of enforceable technical and operational controls that specify how AI agents may interact with data at each level of a classification scheme. Rather than applying a single set of controls to all data, a tiered approach assigns proportionate protections to each sensitivity level—from freely usable public data to highly restricted regulated data—and enforces those controls automatically at the point of agent execution.

A typical enterprise classification scheme has four tiers: Public, Internal, Confidential, and Restricted. Each tier carries distinct rules governing which AI models may process the data, whether the data may appear in prompts, how it must be encrypted, how long it may be retained in context or logs, and whether a human must review the output before it is acted upon. The rules are implemented as policy code enforced by the agent runtime, not as documentation that developers are expected to remember.

Agentic AI systems collapse traditional data access boundaries. An agent orchestrating a customer-service workflow may retrieve data from a CRM (Internal tier), pull account balance from a financial system (Confidential tier), and reference a regulatory document (Public tier)—all in a single reasoning step. Without tier-specific handling rules, the agent's context window becomes a mixing bowl where controls from different tiers do not apply consistently.

The consequences are significant. If Confidential data from a CRM is logged alongside Public data because the logging rule was applied at the session level rather than the data-tier level, the confidential record is retained in plaintext in a log store with weaker access controls. Classification-tier handling rules prevent this by applying data-specific controls at the granularity of each retrieved artifact, not at the coarser granularity of the overall session.
Low-sensitivity data carries low overhead; high-sensitivity data carries strict controls. Neither is over- or under-protected.
Controls applied by the agent runtime cannot be bypassed by developer oversight or misconfiguration.
Auditors can point to per-tier handling rules as evidence that controls were applied at the appropriate sensitivity level.
Handling rules can mandate that Restricted-tier data is summarised or masked before entering the context window, reducing exposure.
All agent teams inherit the same tier definitions and rules, eliminating per-team interpretation of what 'sensitive data' requires.
Handling rules are only as good as the classification labels on the data. Mislabelled or unlabelled data defeats tier-specific controls.
If data arrives without a label (e.g., from a third-party API), the agent must classify it before applying rules, adding latency.
Higher-tier data retrieved early in an agent's reasoning chain may persist in context when the agent later invokes lower-control tools. Context purging logic is needed.
Strict Restricted-tier rules (human-in-the-loop, no external model) can block valid automation use cases and require business justification to waive.

The following tier definitions and their associated handling rules represent a standard enterprise baseline. Organisations may adjust tier names and boundaries, but the control categories below should appear at every tier.
Data that is already publicly available or intended for public distribution. No access controls, no residency constraints, and no restrictions on which AI models may process it.
Any approved model, including external hosted APIs.
May be included in agent prompts without restriction.
Standard audit log of access is sufficient.
No special retention limits; standard data lifecycle applies.
Published product documentation, marketing materials, public research papers, regulatory texts.
Data that is not public but not individually sensitive. Intended for internal business operations.
Approved external models are permitted with data processing agreements. Prefer internal models for efficiency.
May be included in prompts but must not be forwarded to unapproved external endpoints.
Access log with user and agent identifiers required.
No special retention limits on processed outputs.
Internal wikis, project plans, employee directory entries, internal meeting notes.
Data that, if disclosed, would cause material business harm or privacy violations.
Private deployment only. No external API calls with this data in the payload.
May appear in context but content must not be logged. Use opaque identifiers in logs.
Encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3+). Keys managed by enterprise KMS.
RBAC with least-privilege access. Agent must hold an explicit scope to retrieve Confidential data.
Customer PII, financial reports, trade secrets, HR records, legal privileged communications.
Data subject to regulatory mandates with specific handling requirements that go beyond standard confidentiality controls.
Air-gapped or on-premises inference only. No internet-connected model endpoint.
Summarisation or masking required before context window inclusion. Raw values must not appear in prompts.
Zero retention of raw values in any log, cache, or context store.
Agent outputs derived from Restricted data require human review before downstream action.
HSM-backed key management; key rotation every 90 days.
PHI (HIPAA), cardholder data (PCI-DSS), biometric identifiers, regulated financial data.

attrs.alt: Data classification tier handling rules diagram

content.text: Agents that retrieve data from multiple systems with different sensitivity levels need per-artifact handling rules, not session-level rules.
Healthcare, finance, and government deployments face regulatory mandates that map directly to Tier 4 handling requirements.
Retrieval-augmented generation retrieves documents of varying sensitivity. Each retrieved chunk must carry its tier label for correct handling.
Platforms serving multiple customer organisations need tier-based isolation to prevent cross-tenant data leakage.

Implement handling rules as a policy layer that sits between the agent's tool calls and the underlying data sources. Every retrieved artifact must carry a classification label, and the policy layer enforces the correct controls before allowing the data into the agent's context.
Label all data sources with their maximum classification tier at the source level (CRM system = Confidential, public docs = Public).
Propagate the label on every retrieved artifact: add a classification field to tool response schemas.
Implement a context admission policy in the agent runtime that checks the classification of each artifact before admitting it to the context window.
Route model selection based on the highest-tier data in the current context: if any Confidential artifact is present, route to a private model.
Apply logging rules per artifact: strip content before logging for Confidential and Restricted tiers.
Implement context purging: when the agent's reasoning step completes and no further retrieval is needed, purge Restricted data from the in-memory context.

A healthcare AI company builds a clinical decision-support agent that retrieves data from three sources: a public medical reference database (Public), internal clinical protocols (Internal), and patient EHR records (Restricted). The agent runtime implements a context admission policy that checks the tier label on every retrieved chunk before adding it to the prompt. When the first EHR record is admitted, the policy automatically switches the inference route from a shared cloud model to an on-premises GPT-4 equivalent. Patient values are masked to opaque references in the prompt; the full values are held in a secure lookup table accessible only to the on-premises inference endpoint. All outputs that reference patient data require a clinician to confirm before they are written to the EHR.
Applying controls at the session level rather than the artifact level allows mixed-tier data to be processed under the lowest tier's rules.
When an agent summarises a Confidential document and stores the summary, the summary must inherit the Confidential label. Failing to propagate labels creates unlabelled sensitive data.
Restricted data retrieved early in a workflow that persists in the context window during later lower-sensitivity steps violates the no-retention rule.
Many observability platforms log all tool inputs and outputs by default. This must be overridden for Confidential and Restricted tiers.
If the agent switches to a private model on the first Confidential artifact but later retrieves additional Confidential artifacts, the model must remain on the private routing for the entire session.
Label at the source, propagate automatically:
Data sources should embed classification labels in their API responses; agents should never need to classify data themselves at runtime.
Enforce in the framework, not the application:
Build tier handling rules into the agent framework layer so individual agent implementations cannot bypass them.
Treat the highest-tier item as the floor:
Once any Restricted artifact enters a session, all subsequent handling in that session follows Restricted rules until the session ends or the artifact is purged.
Log every instance of tier escalation in a session for forensic analysis.
Test handling rules with synthetic restricted data:
Use synthetic data labeled as Restricted to test that controls (model routing, logging suppression, human review gates) fire correctly.

Per-artifact classification checking adds latency to every tool call. Mitigate this with label caching: once a data source's tier is established, cache it at the source level rather than re-classifying every artifact. For high-throughput agents processing thousands of documents, build the classification check into an async pre-fetch pipeline that pre-labels artifacts before they are retrieved into context.
Run classification labeling as a background job at ingestion time so runtime classification is a simple label lookup.
Track token counts by tier in the context window to enforce maximum context budgets for high-tier data.
Enterprise data classification platform that applies sensitivity labels to documents, emails, and data in Microsoft 365 environments.
Automated data classification service for S3 data, identifying sensitive data types and labeling them.
Policy engine for enforcing tier-based handling rules in agent runtimes via Rego policy definitions.
Open-source PII detection and anonymisation library; useful for masking Restricted-tier values before prompt inclusion.

The classification label itself is a security control: if labels can be forged or stripped, the entire tier system collapses. Labels must be generated at trusted sources, transmitted over authenticated channels, and verified by the agent runtime before use. Label integrity checks—cryptographic signing of classification metadata—protect against label spoofing attacks where a malicious tool returns data labeled as Public when it is actually Confidential.

Reliability of the handling rules depends on the model routing infrastructure. If the private inference endpoint is unavailable when a Confidential artifact is admitted, the agent must fail the request rather than fall back to an external model. Build circuit-breaker patterns into the model routing layer with explicit fail-closed behavior for tier escalation events.
Tier-based handling rules apply proportionate controls to each sensitivity level rather than forcing a single set of controls on all data.
The four standard tiers—Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted—each carry distinct controls covering model routing, logging, encryption, access, and retention.
Controls must be applied at the artifact level, not the session level, because agents commonly mix data from multiple tiers in a single workflow.
Labels must propagate: any artifact derived from Confidential source data inherits the Confidential label.
Enforcement in the framework layer is more reliable than per-application implementation.
